title: Define and Operationalize Yesterday's Core Product Philosophy and Architecture
description:
Context: Multiple strong sources converge on a coherent product vision for Yesterday as an 'Exploration Company' working at the AI frontier. The company should build an ecosystem of small LLM-powered solutions rather than a monolithic product, position AI as the software itself (not a feature), and use a glass-office monitoring UI pattern where agents do the work and humans supervise. The product's core UX invention — Blueprints — replaces free-form prompting with guided, outcome-oriented interaction. Quality is measured via LLM-as-a-judge and synthetic evals, and the company dogfoods its own tools internally.

Scope: (1) Formally document and socialize the 'fleet of sailboats' product philosophy. (2) Define the core separation architecture with package groups: Core Base, Core LLM, Core Agent/Mind, Runtime Agent, Core Chat, and Core Extensions. (3) Specify the Blueprint UX pattern as a reusable design system component. (4) Establish LLM-as-a-judge and evals as the standard QA mechanism across all products. (5) Enforce dogfooding: all internal company outputs (proposals, reports, communications) must flow through Yesterday's own products. (6) Define the agent-as-frontend UI pattern (glass-office monitoring with live agent activity and human-in-the-loop checkpoints) as the default interface paradigm.

Success Criteria: A written product manifesto and architecture document exists and is agreed upon by the founding team. The core package architecture is implemented in at least one live product. At least one Blueprint interaction pattern is live and user-tested. An eval harness runs on at least one product. Internal operations demonstrably use Yesterday tools for at least three recurring workflows.

Source ideas: 72, 73, 74, 75, 76, 77, 78, 106

title: Build the Yesterday Agent Platform: Memory, DNA, and Multi-Agent Cognitive Architecture
description:
Context: A cluster of strong ideas points toward building a robust, reusable agent platform as Yesterday's technical foundation. This includes a full Agent Definition ('DNA') model covering persona, methodology, and runtime state; a rich memory architecture (episodic, semantic, RAG, situational context); a multi-agent cognitive controller that delegates subtasks to specialized sub-agents; and practical tooling like the Yesteeday CLI for scaffolding new agent projects.

Scope: (1) Specify and implement the Agent DNA model: persona (identity, voice, values), methodology (skills, tools, policies), and runtime state (memory, context window, cognitive loop). (2) Build the memory subsystem: episodic memory (conversation abstracts), semantic memory (K/V facts about user, self, world), RAG tool integration, and a situational context policy engine for memory selection. (3) Implement the cognitive controller pattern — an orchestrator agent that routes subtasks to specialized sub-agents per Anthropic's composable patterns (sequential, routing, orchestrator-subagent). (4) Build the 'Yesteeday CLI' tool that scaffolds new agent/product skeletons with cursor rules, tech stack descriptions, file structure, and setup instructions. (5) Validate the architecture with at least two internal products (e.g., Sid assistant config, company RAG knowledge base).

Success Criteria: Agent DNA model is documented as a spec and implemented in code. Memory subsystem handles at least episodic and semantic memory with a working retrieval policy. A multi-agent demo (e.g., cognitive bias experiment or hotel concierge) runs end-to-end on the platform. The CLI tool generates a runnable project skeleton in under 5 minutes. Two internal Yesterday assistants are live and configured via the platform.

Source ideas: 78, 79, 80, 81, 38, 94, 64, 84

title: Launch and Iterate Yesterday's Client-Facing AI Service Portfolio
description:
Context: Yesterday has a defined but not yet fully operationalized service portfolio spanning AI education/workshops, AI implementation sprints, and change management/governance consulting. The three-tier pricing structure (workshops from €3,000, implementation sprints, strategic governance engagements) is outlined. A structured three-step client onboarding process exists. The cidpartners partnership offers an immediate revenue channel (€1,200–1,440/day). BEULCO is an identified near-term client with mapped use cases.

Scope: (1) Finalize and publish the service portfolio (Leistungen-V1) as a client-facing document/website page, covering: keynotes, management coaching, staff AI basics, department deep-dives, AI strategy workshops, implementation sprints (3-day/3-week/3-month), and change management/governance retainers. (2) Formalize the three-step client onboarding process (client AI opportunity list → joint prioritization → on-site team conversations) as a repeatable playbook. (3) Execute the BEULCO engagement: map and prioritize use cases (QS reports, product catalog, chatbot, sustainability reports), propose at least one pilot implementation. (4) Activate the cidpartners partnership for the Voith workshop engagement. (5) Develop a 2–3 sentence value proposition for outbound pitches to prospects like LR Health & Beauty.

Success Criteria: Service portfolio document is finalized and shared with at least three prospects. BEULCO onboarding process is initiated (at minimum, the AI opportunity list meeting is scheduled). One cidpartners workshop is delivered and invoiced. A standard pitch deck and value proposition are ready for new lead conversations.

Source ideas: 12, 13, 42, 46, 47, 48, 15

title: Develop and Deploy a Suite of AI Demo Products for Client Acquisition and Portfolio Credibility
description:
Context: Yesterday's positioning as an 'Exploration Company' requires a strong, visible portfolio of working demos. Multiple strong ideas converge on concrete demo products across voice AI, multi-agent systems, content transformation, and developer tooling. These demos serve dual purposes: (a) direct client acquisition and proof-of-concept for sales conversations, and (b) internal R&D for refining Yesterday's agent platform.

Scope: Build and deploy the following demos with shareable URLs and documented use cases: (1) AI-powered inbound call agent (OpenAI Realtime API + Twilio) — hair salon demo. (2) Voice-based service documentation system (technician records audio → LLM transcribes and structures → service report sent automatically, built on n8n). (3) Comic Converter: transforms online articles into comics using AI. (4) Multi-agent Hotel Concierge Service demo showcasing coordinated agent handoffs. (5) Scientific Document Assistant with real-time collaboration and intelligent editing. (6) Reverse Image Dataset Generator for automated dataset creation. (7) LibreChat deployment as a privacy-compliant enterprise ChatGPT alternative. (8) n8n AI workflow automation demo for a client-relevant use case. (9) PixelTales live demo at pixeltales.0fo.de.

Success Criteria: Each demo has a live URL, a one-paragraph description, and has been shown to at least one prospect or partner. The full demo portfolio is documented in a single 'Show, Don't Tell' page on the Yesterday website or in a sales deck. At least three demos have been used in active client conversations.

Source ideas: 31, 32, 54, 66, 67, 68, 69, 70, 71

title: Build PixelTales as an AI Social Simulation and Research Platform
description:
Context: PixelTales is Yesterday's most conceptually ambitious product — a platform where AI characters interact, can be directed into scenarios, and serve as a laboratory for humanoid AI research. It has identified value in entertainment, psychological/social research simulation, and marketing. The product is partially live (pixeltales.0fo.de) but needs a clear development roadmap to realize its research and commercial potential.

Scope: (1) Define the core interaction model: AI characters with parameterized mental subsystems (persona, emotional state, memory, behavior policies) that can be configured for different experimental conditions. (2) Build the psychological simulation layer: enable replication of classic studies (anchoring bias, social conformity, etc.) by orchestrating LLM agents as experimenter, subject, and analyst via LangGraph. (3) Build the 'mind editor' UI: a control-room-style visualization of AI mental subsystems, with toggleable components and live state display. (4) Define and document the platform's research use cases (human empathy studies, AI alignment probing, social dynamics simulation) and entertainment use cases (interactive storytelling, character-driven content). (5) Publish at least one documented 'experiment' — a cognitive bias study run entirely on PixelTales — as a thought leadership piece.

Success Criteria: PixelTales supports at least 3 configurable character parameters (personality, memory policy, emotional state). One documented social simulation experiment is run and published. The mind editor UI is functional in at least a prototype state. The platform is stable enough to demo live in a sales or event context.

Source ideas: 60, 61, 62, 83, 94

title: Build the Yesterday Multi-Tenant AI Assistant Platform (OpenAI Assistant Demo Evolution)
description:
Context: The OpenAI Assistant Demo is an existing internal product that needs to evolve into a production-grade, multi-tenant platform. This platform would serve as both an internal tool and a client-deliverable white-label solution. Key missing capabilities include SSO, client management, subscription handling, admin dashboards, response validation (branding, safety, quality scoring), and reporting.

Scope: (1) Implement multi-tenancy: client isolation, SSO integration, and client management UI. (2) Build admin dashboard: performance reporting, usage metrics, and assistant configuration management. (3) Implement response validation pipeline: racism/toxicity detection, brand voice consistency scoring, friendliness scoring. (4) Implement structured outputs using Zod widget schemas and plugin-registered widget signatures with global store for widget data. (5) Add Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration for standardized context provision to LLMs, including Git and Puppeteer browser use servers. (6) Set up CI/CD with GitHub Actions and basic Prometheus monitoring. (7) Position the platform as a deployable enterprise AI assistant offering in the Yesterday service portfolio.

Success Criteria: The platform supports at least two isolated tenants with separate configurations. Admin dashboard shows usage and quality metrics. Response validation pipeline runs on 100% of outputs and flags policy violations. MCP integration is demonstrated with at least one server (Git or Puppeteer). Platform is used in at least one paying client deployment.

Source ideas: 56, 57, 30, 59

title: Establish Yesterday's AI Workshop Curriculum Including a Bias and Ethics Module
description:
Context: AI education and workshops are a core revenue stream and a brand-building mechanism for Yesterday. The workshop portfolio is defined but content needs to be built, and specific high-value modules (like training bias) are explicitly identified. The AI Jam/Hackathon format is a complementary community engagement and lead-generation event format. The team also needs a structured internal learning curriculum to stay technically current.

Scope: (1) Build out workshop content for each tier: keynote (60-90 min), management coaching (half day, €3,000), staff AI basics (full day, from €4,000), department deep-dives (full day, €5,000), AI strategy workshop (full day, €4,000). (2) Create a dedicated 'Training Bias in AI' module: demonstrate how pre-training data skews model outputs using reproducible examples (clocks at 10:10, right-handedness, pet distribution), suitable for inclusion in any tier. (3) Design and run one AI Jam / Hackathon event: participants bring micro-project ideas, build and deploy in one day, with interim and final presentations. (4) Build an internal team learning curriculum covering Python/Flask, React, LLM fundamentals, and Data Science basics — structured as a 12-week program with weekly sessions.

Success Criteria: All five workshop tiers have slide decks and facilitator guides ready. The training bias module is tested with at least one internal audience. One AI Jam event is run with at least 8 external participants. The internal learning curriculum is scheduled and the first four sessions are completed.

Source ideas: 46, 63, 24, 4

title: Build and Operate an AI-Assisted Crypto Trading System with Freqtrade and Signal Intelligence
description:
Context: The company has an existing interest in and partial setup for AI-powered crypto trading. Freqtrade with FreqAI (self-learning bot) is the identified platform. The vision extends to an LLM-based signal intelligence layer that predicts short/long/uncertain signals with confidence levels and automates trade execution via 3Commas.

Scope: (1) Set up and stabilize Freqtrade instances: one standard strategy bot and one FreqAI self-learning bot, both backtested against Binance data using Polygon.io as the data provider. (2) Build or integrate an LLM-based signal layer: prompt-based chart analysis (via screenshot ingestion) that outputs structured trade signals (short/long/uncertain + confidence %). (3) Integrate with 3Commas (or equivalent) for automated trade execution based on signal confidence thresholds. (4) Build a simple monitoring dashboard: live P&L, signal log, trade history, and bot health status. (5) Define and document risk parameters: maximum position size, confidence thresholds for execution, stop-loss rules.

Success Criteria: At least one Freqtrade bot is running live (not just in backtest) with documented strategy and risk parameters. The LLM signal layer produces structured outputs for at least 20 historical chart inputs with measurable accuracy vs. actual price movement. 3Commas integration is tested in paper trading mode. A monitoring dashboard is accessible and updated in real time.

Source ideas: 51, 52, 53

title: Build a Personal and Organizational Knowledge Management System with AI Assistance
description:
Context: Two strong ideas converge on the need for a systematic personal knowledge management (PKM) system and a daily brainstorming/capture routine. The company also maintains a growing knowledge base of AI/ML frameworks and tools. These are related problems: the founder needs personal knowledge infrastructure, and the company needs a curated, searchable technical reference base. Both benefit from AI-assisted capture, categorization, and retrieval.

Scope: (1) Set up an Obsidian-based PKM system with a Zettelkasten-style structure: atomic notes, tags, backlinks, and a daily note template for the brainstorming session routine. (2) Establish a daily 'brain dump' session of 15–20 minutes: capture thoughts, ideas, and tasks using voice-to-text or mobile input, with real-time sync and versioning (e.g., via Git or Obsidian Sync). (3) Configure an AI assistant plugin (e.g., Smart Connections or a custom LLM integration) for auto-completion, cross-referencing, and surfacing related notes. (4) Maintain a dedicated section for the AI/ML framework knowledge base (NLP, agents, RAG, automation, orchestration, monitoring, deployment tools) — updated monthly and used as a client and internal project reference. (5) Define a personal values hierarchy across seven life domains (work, friendships, creativity, leisure, health, family, love) as a permanent reference node in the PKM.

Success Criteria: PKM system is set up and actively used daily for at least 30 consecutive days. Daily capture routine has a documented template and is adhered to ≥5 days/week. AI assistant surfaces at least one useful cross-reference per week during active sessions. The AI/ML framework knowledge base has at least 40 entries organized by category and is reviewed/updated at least once per month. Values hierarchy document is completed and reviewed quarterly.

Source ideas: 6, 7, 21, 29

title: Write and Publish a Thought Leadership Essay on the AI Adoption Gap
description:
Context: The founder has a strong, clearly articulated perspective on AI's societal role: the 'last mile of AI' mission, the importance of broad public engagement before superintelligence arrives, the risk of AI being weaponized against democracy, and the observation that companies fall into distinct categories in their response to the AI wave. This intellectual capital should be converted into a published essay that builds the Yesterday brand, attracts clients, and contributes to public discourse on responsible AI adoption.

Scope: (1) Draft a 1,500–2,500 word essay (publishable under pseudonym or company name) covering: the AI adoption gap (who is engaging vs. who is not), a taxonomy of company 'reactor types' to the AI wave, the risk of pre-superintelligent AI being used as a political weapon, and a call to action for broad public engagement with AI tools. (2) Incorporate the 'In a world where the cost of answers drops to zero, the value of a question becomes everything' framing. (3) Include the AI alignment perspective: creating touchpoints that inspire, excite, and prompt reflection as a contribution to alignment. (4) Publish on at least two channels (e.g., LinkedIn + Medium or Substack). (5) Track engagement metrics (reads, shares, inbound contacts generated).

Success Criteria: Essay is drafted, edited, and published within 6 weeks. It is distributed on at least two channels. It generates at least one inbound conversation (lead, media inquiry, or partnership interest) within 30 days of publication. The essay is linked from Yesterday's website as a 'Perspectives' piece.

Source ideas: 108, 107, 9, 19, 72
